brief_document.title: General key-value API
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the general key-value API (get, put, delete, range, commit) that wraps the core shard actor operations with routing, error handling, and the public type surface. This is the foundational API that both the EventStore and TTL layers build on.
brief_document.task: Implement the KV API operations routing through the shard router: get checks buffer then tree, put appends to buffer, delete appends tombstone, range merges buffer and tree in sorted order, commit flushes all shard WAL buffers. These are the same operations the shard actor handles internally; this brief wraps them with the public API surface, shard routing, and error types. Does not implement EventStore operations (API-001) or TTL (API-003).

requirements.title: KV API module
requirements.spec: Create the kv.rs module exposing get, put, delete, range, and commit operations on the database handle. Each operation routes to the correct shard via hash(key) % shard_count.
acceptance: All five operations are publicly accessible from the database handle
Each operation routes to the correct shard actor

requirements.title: Get operation
requirements.spec: WHEN get(key) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL route to the owning shard and return Option<Value>. THE SYSTEM SHALL check the shard's WAL buffer first, then the tree. THE SYSTEM SHALL return None for keys that do not exist.
acceptance: get after put returns the written value
get on a non-existent key returns None
get returns the buffer value even if the tree has a stale value for the same key

requirements.title: Put and delete operations
requirements.spec: WHEN put(key, value) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL append the mutation to the owning shard's WAL buffer. WHEN delete(key) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL append a tombstone to the owning shard's WAL buffer. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT flush to the tree — that is commit()'s job.
acceptance: put(key, value) followed by get(key) returns the value
delete(key) followed by get(key) returns None
put does not trigger a tree commit
delete does not trigger a tree commit

requirements.title: Range query
requirements.spec: WHEN range(from, to) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL return key-value pairs in sorted key order, merging buffer entries with tree entries. THE SYSTEM SHALL route the range to the correct shard(s) based on key hash. Buffer entries shadow tree entries for the same key.
acceptance: range returns entries in sorted key order
range includes entries from both the buffer and the tree
A buffered put shadows a tree entry for the same key in range results
A buffered delete excludes the key from range results

requirements.title: Commit operation
requirements.spec: WHEN commit() is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL flush all shard WAL buffers to their respective prolly trees and return per-shard root hashes. THE SYSTEM SHALL produce exactly one tree commit per shard.
acceptance: commit() returns a map of shard_id to root_hash
After commit, get returns values from the tree (buffer is empty)
Each shard produces exactly one tree commit
Calling commit() with no buffered writes is a no-op that returns current root hashes

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement EventStore operations — those are API-001
SHALL NOT implement TTL or expiry — that is API-003
SHALL NOT implement cross-shard range queries that span multiple shards in a single sorted result — range routes to the owning shard(s)
SHALL NOT implement batch put/delete — single-key operations only at this layer
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests
cargo clippy -p haematite produces no errors
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/api/ --include='*.rs' -l finds no non-test files
No file in crates/haematite/src/api/ exceeds 500 lines

adrs.title: WAL buffer for write amortization rather than direct tree mutation
adrs.decision: WAL buffer over direct tree mutation, because it gives sequential-append write latency (the WAL) with amortized tree cost (one path-to-root rewrite per flush, not per write). The rejected alternative (direct mutation with batching windows) forces callers to accept high write latency or risk high write amplification with small batches.

adrs.title: Actor-per-shard with fixed shard count at creation
adrs.decision: Actor-per-shard on beamr with fixed shard count at creation, over single-writer and lock-per-shard, because the actor model eliminates locks entirely — each shard is a single-writer beamr process with no shared mutable state. Fixed shard count over dynamic resharding because resharding is operationally complex and can be deferred: 256 or 1024 shards covers any reasonable single-node deployment for Aion's workload (one key per workflow_id).

checklist.text: get(key) returns Option<Value>, checking the shard's WAL buffer first, then the tree.
put(key, value) appends to the shard's WAL buffer.
delete(key) appends a tombstone to the shard's WAL buffer.
range(from, to) merges buffer and tree entries in sorted order.
commit() flushes all shard WAL buffers and returns per-shard root hashes.

stories.text: As the messaging bus, I want to put and get conversation state by key so that durable channel state survives restarts.
As the messaging bus, I want to delete keys so that I can clean up closed conversations.
As the messaging bus, I want range queries to return keys in sorted order so that I can efficiently list conversations in a channel.
As an application developer, I want a simple get/put/delete/range API so that I can use haematite without understanding prolly trees or WAL buffers.
As an application developer, I want commit to flush all pending writes and return root hashes so that I can control the durability boundary.

constraints.text: append with expected_seq must be atomic: the sequence check and the append happen within the shard actor's message handler with no interleaving.
Batch append of N events must produce exactly one tree commit, not N individual commits.
Expired entries must never be returned by get, read, read_from, range, or scan — regardless of sweep actor state.
The sweep actor must not block the shard actor's write path. Sweep runs as a separate supervised process.
scan traverses all shards. The caller must understand this is O(total entries across all shards), not O(entries in one shard).
cas is atomic within a shard but provides no cross-shard guarantees.

resolved_context.intention: The API cluster is the contract between haematite and every application that embeds it. When this cluster is complete, Aion can use haematite as its EventStore backend with append/read/read_from/scan/cas operations, the messaging bus can use general KV operations for durable channel state, and both get built-in TTL expiry without application-level sweep logic. The API should feel like a thin, honest surface — every operation maps directly to the underlying shard actor, nothing is hidden behind clever abstractions, and the cost model is transparent. A caller who understands the shard model should be able to predict the performance of any API call.

provenance.quote: The API cluster is the contract between haematite and every application that embeds it. When this cluster is complete, Aion can use haematite as its EventStore backend with append/read/read_from/scan/
